title: segment_sum/segment_mean missing adgCMatrix branch, densifies
description:
## Location
R/wrappers.R:917-942

## Problem
segment_sum() and segment_mean() check for adgeMatrix but not adgCMatrix. Any sparse input falls through to .am_segment_sum_cpu(as.matrix(x), ...) which densifies the entire matrix. The C-level sparse segment sum (am_sparse_segment_sum_c) already exists and is wired through rowsum.adgCMatrix — but segment_sum() does not use it.

## Fix
Add adgCMatrix branch before the densification fallback:
  if (inherits(x, "adgCMatrix")) return(.am_sparse_segment_sum_cpu(x, labels, K))
where .am_sparse_segment_sum_cpu calls the existing C routine or rowsum.adgCMatrix.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] segment_sum(adgCMatrix, labels, K) does NOT call as.matrix()
- [ ] segment_mean(adgCMatrix, labels, K) does NOT call as.matrix()
- [ ] Results match dense reference at tolerance 1e-14
- [ ] Test with 10K×1K sparse at 1% density, K=100 groups — no memory spike
- [ ] All existing tests pass

title: Validate MLX GPU Cholesky plus batched solve on flagship SPD workflows
description: Turn the existing MLX bridge-backed Cholesky and triangular-solve path into a benchmarked, trustworthy second flagship. Scope: add focused parity tests around am_chol_factor() and am_chol_solve() in fast MLX mode; benchmark representative SPD many-RHS workloads such as ridge-like solves or kernel systems; document where the MLX path is already real and where ArrayFire remains a stub or fallback.
acceptance_criteria: MLX factor and solve paths have benchmark-backed validation; docs describe the Cholesky workflow honestly as implemented and validated, not merely planned; any remaining backend gaps are explicit
notes: Completed with native-MLX parity and benchmark coverage. Added focused fast-MLX package tests for ridge-like and kernel-like SPD many-RHS solves plus backend-local chol smoke coverage. Reliable benchmark invocation on this machine is: Rscript -e "source(\"tools/benchmark-cholesky-runtime.R\", local = TRUE)". Current spot results: ridge_spd 768x768 rhs=64 factor+solve cpu 0.079s vs mlx 0.028s; kernel_spd 640x640 rhs=32 factor+solve cpu 0.042s vs mlx 0.016s. Reconstruction residuals are about 2e-7 and solve errors about 5e-7 versus CPU reference. ArrayFire remains explicit CPU fallback/stub territory for this workflow.

title: Replace src_id transpose shortcut with proper aTransposeView
description: Current state: the shipped resident transpose shortcut keeps t(A) %*% B and A %*% t(B) on the fast resident path by returning a src_id-linked adgeMatrix with transposed host data. That fixes the re-upload problem, but it is still not a true structural view and it pays O(nm) host transpose cost and memory.\n\nGoal: introduce a dedicated Matrix-compatible aTransposeView (or broader aMatrixView family) that carries source linkage and orientation without pretending to be concrete dense storage.
acceptance_criteria:
t(A) on dense matrices returns a dedicated structural view rather than a materialized adgeMatrix
t(A) %*% B and A %*% t(B) still reuse the existing crossprod/tcrossprod resident fast paths
t(t(A)) collapses cleanly without redundant host transposition
as.matrix() or explicit coercion materializes correctly when a concrete dense value is required
The view carries distinct cache and residency identity from the source matrix
notes: This is the real next iteration after the shipped src_id shortcut. It should stay narrow: transpose view only, no scaled views, no deferred expression fusion. It also sets up ALTREP-facing host-materialization work more naturally later.
close_reason: Implemented: aTransposeView S4 class, zero-copy t(), t(t(A)) collapse, %*% dispatch to crossprod/tcrossprod. 882 tests pass.

title: woodbury_solve(): Woodbury identity for O(nk^2) rank-k system updates
Sparse solve/qr audit: route adgCMatrix to Matrix CHOLMOD without densification
description:
## Problem
solve(adgCMatrix, b) and qr(adgCMatrix) currently call as.matrix() before dispatch,
densifying the matrix. Matrix package has CHOLMOD-backed sparse solve and SuiteSparse
sparse QR that handle dgCMatrix natively.

## Scope
Sparse solve and QR are uncommon on the main hot paths (LDA/k-means/PCA all use
crossprod/matmul, not solve), but they matter for graph Laplacian systems and sparse
regression pipelines. This is an audit + targeted fix, not a full reimplementation.

## Changes

### solve(adgCMatrix, b) — R/methods-sparse.R
Current:
  setMethod('solve', signature(a='adgCMatrix', b='missing'), ...)  → am_solve(a)
  setMethod('solve', signature(a='adgCMatrix', b='ANY'),     ...)  → am_solve(a, b)

am_solve dispatches to backend which calls as.matrix(). Fix:
In am_solve (R/wrappers.R), add sparse guard before dispatch:
  if (inherits(x_host, 'sparseMatrix')) {
    if (is.null(b)) return(Matrix::solve(x_host))        # sparse matrix inverse — rare
    return(as.matrix(Matrix::solve(x_host, b)))          # sparse linear system — common
  }

Note: sparse matrix inverse (b=NULL) is dense and huge — warn if ncol > 500.

### qr(adgCMatrix) — R/methods-sparse.R
Current: setMethod('qr', 'adgCMatrix', ...) → am_qr which calls as.matrix().
Matrix::qr(dgCMatrix) returns a sparseQR object (SuiteSparse). However, the amDenseQR
class is used downstream (qr.Q, qr.R, qr.coef etc.). 

Safest approach: don't change qr dispatch; instead add a note that sparse QR
materializes to dense (document in am_qr). Only route b-present solve to sparse.

### Audit checklist
Run this and check for unexpected as.matrix calls on sparse inputs:
  grep -n 'as\.matrix' R/wrappers.R R/backend-cpu.R | grep -v '#'
For each hit, verify whether it's reachable from an adgCMatrix input.

## Verification
  library(Matrix); set.seed(1)
  # Sparse linear system: X is square sparse, b is dense vector
  X = adgCMatrix(rsparsematrix(500, 500, density=0.05) + 50*diag(500))
  b = rnorm(500)
  x_sol  = solve(X, b)
  x_ref  = as.vector(Matrix::solve(amatrix_materialize_host(X), b))
  cat('solve err:', max(abs(x_sol - x_ref)), '\n')
  devtools::test()  # 1141+ pass

title: Speed up resident t() products via src_id transpose shortcut
description: Current state: resident dense transpose products no longer force a device re-upload. t() on a resident adgeMatrix returns a src_id-linked adgeMatrix with correctly transposed host data, and am_matmul() routes t(A) %*% B and A %*% t(B) through crossprod_resident()/tcrossprod_resident() when possible.\n\nScope of this completed step: ship the narrow fast-path shortcut first, keep semantics correct, and avoid introducing a public lazy algebra DSL.
acceptance_criteria:
Resident t(A) %*% B reuses the source residency through the crossprod resident path
Resident A %*% t(B) reuses the source residency through the tcrossprod resident path
Correctness matches the previous eager materialized behavior
No general deferred expression system is introduced
notes: Implemented as a stepping stone. Follow-up structural-view work should live in a separate open issue rather than mutating this completed shortcut into the final object-model design.
close_reason: Implemented: src_id slot on aMatrix, zero-copy t() for resident matrices, fast-path in am_matmul for t(A)%*%B and A%*%t(B). 5 new tests, 846 pass.

title: Add trace(A) matrix trace
description:
sum(diag(A)) as a named GPU-dispatched function. Currently requires sum(diag(as.matrix(A))) which breaks residency. Needed constantly in REML gradients, matrix calculus, and covariance identities.

Scope:
- trace(A) returns scalar double = sum(diag(A))
- Register S4 setMethod on adgeMatrix
- GPU path: extract diagonal then sum (avoids full materialization for large matrices)
- Tests: matches sum(diag(as.matrix(A))), works on non-square matrices (min(n,p) diagonal)
close_reason: trace(A) added in R/wrappers.R. Scalar sum(diag(A)) with GPU materialization. 957 tests pass.

title: amatrix_dispatch_op drops resident binding unconditionally on cold path
description:
## Location
R/backend-planning.R:200-204

## Problem
amatrix_dispatch_op unconditionally drops the resident GPU binding and materializes to host before calling the backend method — even when the chosen backend IS a GPU backend. For ops that have dedicated _try_resident_* helpers (matmul, crossprod, ewise, etc.) this is OK because the resident path is tried first. But for svd, eigen, qr, diag, and broadcast_ewise cold paths, if a GPU backend is chosen but lacks a resident helper, the data gets pulled to host and the resident binding is destroyed. If the operation chains with a subsequent GPU op, the matrix must be re-uploaded.

Critical for irlba: if the resident matmul fast path ever falls through to dispatch_op, the full matrix is re-uploaded on EVERY Lanczos step — catastrophic for large matrices.

## Fix
amatrix_dispatch_op should check whether the chosen backend can operate on the resident key directly before dropping the binding. If the backend has a matching resident op, use it. Only materialize to host as a last resort.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] amatrix_dispatch_op does NOT call .amatrix_drop_resident_binding() when the backend supports resident ops for the given operation
- [ ] GPU-resident matrix passed through dispatch_op for a supported op stays resident
- [ ] GPU-resident matrix passed through dispatch_op for an unsupported op correctly falls back to host
- [ ] No regression in any existing test (1156+)
- [ ] Audit: grep for .amatrix_drop_resident_binding confirms it is only called when necessary

title: SpGeMM: sparse×sparse multiply via Matrix pkg fallback
description:
## Problem
adgCMatrix %*% adgCMatrix currently calls matmul() which routes to the backend's
matmul method. The CPU backend does as.matrix(x) %*% as.matrix(y) — densifying
both operands. For graph algorithms that need A^2 = A %*% A (graph squaring,
reachability, diffusion), this is catastrophically expensive.

Matrix package has compiled SpGeMM via CHOLMOD (sparse × sparse → sparse result).
We just need to not densify.

## Scope
GPU SpGeMM (AF or MLX sparse×sparse) is not supported and would require custom
kernels — out of scope here. This issue is purely about the CPU fallback: ensure
adgCMatrix %*% adgCMatrix routes to Matrix::crossprod / Matrix::'%*%' instead of
densifying, and wraps the result back as adgCMatrix.

## Changes

### R/backend-cpu.R — matmul
The CPU backend's matmul currently does x %*% y where x is dgCMatrix.
x %*% y where y is also a dgCMatrix already works via Matrix dispatch (SpGeMM).
But y arrives as the raw R arg (host form), not necessarily dgCMatrix — it could
be a plain matrix. Add a check:
  matmul = function(x, y) {
    if (inherits(x, 'sparseMatrix') && inherits(y, 'sparseMatrix')) {
      return(as(x %*% y, 'matrix'))   # or keep sparse? see note
    }
    if (inherits(x, 'sparseMatrix')) return(as(x %*% y, 'matrix'))
    ...existing dense path...
  }

Note on result type: sparse × sparse can produce a sparse result (e.g. A^2 for
sparse A). For now, convert to dense and wrap as adgCMatrix if density < threshold
(say 0.5), otherwise adgeMatrix. Or always return dense — simpler, matches current
contract that matmul returns a dense-ish matrix.

### .amatrix_host_arg (R/wrappers.R)
When y is an adgCMatrix, .amatrix_host_arg(y) should return dgCMatrix not dense matrix.
Check current behaviour: grep -n '_host_arg' R/wrappers.R | head -10
If it calls as.matrix(), fix it to return dgCMatrix for sparse inputs.

### S4 dispatch for %*%(adgCMatrix, adgCMatrix)
Already has setMethod('%*%', signature(x='adgCMatrix', y='adgCMatrix'), ...) in
methods-sparse.R — this calls matmul(x, y). The fix in backend-cpu.R above should
propagate through.

## Verification
  library(Matrix); set.seed(1)
  A = adgCMatrix(rsparsematrix(1000, 1000, density=0.01))
  B = adgCMatrix(rsparsematrix(1000, 500,  density=0.01))
  # SpGeMM: A %*% B should not densify A or B
  R1 = as.matrix(A %*% B)
  R2 = as.matrix(amatrix_materialize_host(A) %*% amatrix_materialize_host(B))
  cat('SpGeMM err:', max(abs(R1 - R2)), '\n')
  # A^2 (graph squaring)
  A2 = A %*% A
  cat('A^2 class:', class(A2), '\n')
  devtools::test()  # 1141+ pass

title: Add explicit am_gemm() public function
description: Expose am_gemm(A, B, C=NULL, alpha=1, beta=1, transA=FALSE, transB=FALSE) as a documented power-user function. Keeps %*% boring and eager (no contract change). AF has native af_gemm; MLX composes mlx_matmul + scaled mlx_add. Files: R/wrappers.R, both backend.R files, NAMESPACE. Tests: am_gemm(A,B) == A %*% B, transA/transB variants, non-NULL C accumulator.

title: Fused am_cov/am_cor: eliminate GPU round-trips in covariance chain
description: Currently center→tcrossprod→scale incurs 2 unnecessary host↔device transfers (centering on CPU, tcrossprod on GPU, scaling on CPU). Add am_cov() and am_cor() that keep the full chain on device. PRD ref: docs/prd.json:611-615. Files: R/wrappers.R (public API), C bridge for fused center+tcrossprod. Target workloads: fMRI, genomics, ridge paths. Expected 2-5x speedup on those workflows.
close_reason: Fused covariance bridge implemented in C (amatrix_mlx_covariance_bridge); am_covariance() dispatches to GPU path eliminating both host materializations. 862 tests pass.

title: Block Lanczos GPU-native truncated SVD (batched GEMM irlba upgrade)
description: Replace the current per-step GEMV Lanczos compatibility path with a GEMM-oriented block Lanczos implementation on MLX after the flagship QR and Cholesky workflows are benchmarked and documented cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: Block Lanczos shows a credible benchmark-backed win over the current am_irlba compatibility path; the work does not pre-empt unfinished flagship workflow hardening
notes: Completed on 2026-04-06 and refined further the same day. Added am_block_lanczos() in R/irlba.R, kept am_block_svd() as the alias surface, and wired am_irlba(..., implementation = 'block') to the new path. Focused coverage lives in tests/testthat/test-block-lanczos.R and the source-driven harnesses live in tools/benchmark-block-lanczos.R and tools/profile-block-lanczos.R. Profiling first identified the repeated MLX resident crossprod transpose as the main loss; the block path now builds a resident-transposed MLX right operator once per solve and uses direct resident matmul helpers on both the left and right products. A second pass then removed unnecessary final global QR work by skipping it whenever the concatenated block bases are already orthonormal within tolerance. Re-verified from source: testthat::test_file('tests/testthat/test-block-lanczos.R') passes. Current benchmark results on this Apple Silicon MLX setup: 1200x600, k=10, block_size=12, n_steps=4 -> irlba::irlba 0.009s, am_irlba 0.219s, am_block_lanczos 0.018s with max_rel_sv_err 0.0468; 3000x1200, k=20, block_size=24, n_steps=4 -> irlba::irlba 0.126s, am_irlba 0.487s, am_block_lanczos 0.091s with max_rel_sv_err 0.0479; 5000x2000, k=20, block_size=24, n_steps=4 -> irlba::irlba 0.421s, am_irlba 0.715s, am_block_lanczos 0.147s with max_rel_sv_err 0.0462. The surface remains explicitly approximate and should not be described as a strict replacement for CPU irlba yet.

title: resident_handle: mutable GPU handle for iterative workloads
description: Implemented resident_handle (R/resident-handle.R): lightweight mutable environment wrapping a GPU-resident key. Operations mutate in-place (am_sweep_inplace, am_ewise_inplace, rh_rowSums, rh_colSums), avoiding S4 allocation per step. 15.8x faster than base R on 2000x2000 Sinkhorn (vs 3-4x slower with adgeMatrix). Also delivered: kernel_matrix preferred_backend+zero_diag (GPU-resident kernel with on-device diagonal zeroing), deferred host materialization infrastructure (internal, not user-facing).

title: trace()/sym() and CPU backend linalg ops densify sparse unconditionally
description:
## Locations
- R/wrappers.R:840-843 (trace)
- R/wrappers.R:848-851 (sym)
- R/backend-cpu.R:67-87 (solve, chol, qr, svd, eigen, diag)

## Problem — trace()
Materializes full matrix via as.matrix(amatrix_materialize_host(x)) just to extract diagonal and sum. For sparse, Matrix::diag(dgCMatrix) extracts diagonal in O(nnz) without densifying. For a 100K×100K sparse matrix, this creates a 80GB dense copy.

## Problem — sym()
Calls .amatrix_model_dense_arg which densifies any sparse input. (X + t(X))/2 works natively on dgCMatrix via Matrix package.

## Problem — CPU backend linalg
solve/chol/qr/svd/eigen/diag all call as.matrix(x) unconditionally. The am_* wrappers have sparse guards for adgCMatrix, but if dispatch routes to the CPU backend directly (e.g., for raw dgCMatrix), these densify silently.

## Fix
1. trace(): use sum(Matrix::diag(host)) for sparse; sum(diag(host)) for dense
2. sym(): add sparse path: host <- amatrix_materialize_host(x); (host + Matrix::t(host)) / 2
3. CPU backend: add inherits(x, "sparseMatrix") guards routing to Matrix:: equivalents

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] trace(adgCMatrix) does NOT call as.matrix() — uses Matrix::diag
- [ ] sym(adgCMatrix) does NOT call as.matrix() — result is adgCMatrix
- [ ] CPU backend solve/chol/qr on sparseMatrix uses Matrix:: methods
- [ ] All results match dense reference at tolerance 1e-10
- [ ] All existing tests pass

title: amatrix.opencl: custom OpenCL kernels (ewise, broadcast_sweep, reductions)
description: Write and compile 7 custom OpenCL kernels: ewise_add, ewise_sub, ewise_div, scalar_mul, broadcast_sweep (margin 1/2), row_sum, col_sum. Compile once at first use via clCreateProgramWithSource, cache cl_program/cl_kernel handles. Both cold-path and _resident variants. Required for resident_handle Sinkhorn loop. See docs/plan-amatrix-opencl.md.

title: MLX rowSums_resident/colSums_resident materialize to host instead of using GPU kernel
description:
## Location
backends/amatrix.mlx/R/backend.R:709-716

## Problem
MLX rowSums_resident and colSums_resident materialize the GPU-resident matrix to host via amatrix_materialize_host(), then call base::rowSums/base::colSums on CPU. The MLX backend already has amatrix_mlx_axis_sums (GPU reduction kernel) but the resident versions do not call it. Every rowsums()/colsums() on a GPU-resident MLX matrix pays a full GPU→CPU transfer + CPU reduction instead of a pure GPU reduction.

## Fix
Route rowSums_resident/colSums_resident through the existing amatrix_mlx_axis_sums bridge, passing the resident array directly without materializing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] rowSums on GPU-resident MLX matrix does NOT call amatrix_materialize_host()
- [ ] colSums on GPU-resident MLX matrix does NOT call amatrix_materialize_host()
- [ ] Results match base::rowSums/colSums reference at tolerance 1e-6 (f32 GPU)
- [ ] Benchmark: rowSums on GPU-resident 5000x1000 is ≥1.5x faster than host fallback
- [ ] All existing tests pass

title: amatrix.opencl: CLBlast GEMM/SYRK wrappers (matmul, crossprod, tcrossprod)

Use CLBlastSsyrk for self-crossprod/tcrossprod + sym_fill kernel to copy untouched triangle (amatrix expects full matrix). Both cold-path and _resident variants with defer parameter. See docs/plan-amatrix-opencl.md.

title: Add am_rowscale(X, d) and am_colscale(X, d) diagonal scaling without breaking residency
description:
Scale rows or columns of a matrix by a vector without pulling a resident matrix to host. Currently X / d or X * d in base R breaks GPU residency, forcing a round-trip on every call. This is needed for:

- EMMA inner loop: D^{-1} X* = X* scaled by 1/d (row-wise) stays resident for repeated crossprod calls
- Preconditioning: scale rows/cols before a solve
- Standardization: divide columns by standard deviations
- Any diagonal-times-matrix or matrix-times-diagonal op

API:
  am_rowscale(X, d)   # diag(d) %*% X  — scale each row i by d[i]
  am_colscale(X, d)   # X %*% diag(d)  — scale each col j by d[j]

Both return an adgeMatrix with the same backend/precision as X.

Backend:
- CPU fallback: X * d (row) or t(t(X) * d) (col)
- MLX: element-wise multiply keeping the array resident; no host round-trip
- Result should inherit residency from X when possible

Relationship to am_ewise: am_ewise handles scalar and same-shape ops but not broadcast-along-axis. This fills that gap without introducing a general broadcasting DSL.
close_reason: Implemented am_rowscale(X,d) and am_colscale(X,d) in R/wrappers.R. CPU path via host materialisation + rewrap. 25 new tests pass including EMMA rotation pattern test (rowscale + crossprod == crossprod_weighted). GPU-resident path is a future optimisation.

title: Add trace_estim(A, k) stochastic Hutchinson trace estimator
description:
Approximates tr(A) via k Rademacher random probes without materializing A or A^{-1}. Enables REML gradients tr(V^{-1} dV/dθ) at O(nk) cost instead of O(n^3).

Algorithm: E[z' A z] = tr(A) for z ~ Rademacher(±1). Estimate with k probes.
For tr(K^{-1}): supply a solve_fn = function(v) chol_solve(L, v) — probe is z' K^{-1} z.

API:
  trace_estim(A, k = 30, seed = NULL)
  trace_estim(solve_fn, n, k = 30, seed = NULL)  # for implicit A via function

For trace_estim(solve_fn, n, k): generates k random ±1 vectors of length n,
  applies solve_fn to each, computes mean of z' solve_fn(z).

Uses: REML gradient tr(P V_i), log-det approximation, kernel learning.

title: Normalize sparse QR return contract
description: Context: sparse solve/chol/covariance now return amatrix wrappers, but qr(adgCMatrix) still returns raw Matrix sparseQR. Scope: decide the intended sparse QR contract, implement a wrapped return that matches the rest of amatrix's factor API or document and test the exception explicitly. Acceptance: sparse QR behavior is intentional, tested, and consistent with the public API contract rather than leaking an unwrapped Matrix type by accident.

title: Symmetric eigendecomposition GPU bridge (mlx_linalg_eigh)
MLX C++ sparse residency: native mlx::core::array storage across SpMM calls
description:
## Problem
amatrix-8z6 (completed) implemented MLX sparse SpMM with R-level caching — the dgCMatrix
slots are cached in R and passed to the C++ bridge on each call. However, each call still
converts @x/@p/@i → mlx::core::array on the C++ side, paying the conversion cost every
time. For irlba (O(k) Lanczos steps), this is O(k) conversions of the same NNZ values.

## Goal
Build a true C++ sparse residency cache: upload the mlx sparse arrays once, cache
mlx::core::array handles by key, and reuse them across SpMM calls.

## MLX sparse API
MLX represents sparse arrays as a triple (values: array, row_indices: array, col_ptrs: array)
with shape annotation. The mlx::core::sparse_mm call takes mlx arrays directly.
Caching the arrays avoids reconstructing them each call.

## Implementation

### C++ registry (amatrix_mlx_matmul.cpp or new amatrix_mlx_sparse.cpp)
Parallel to the AF sparse registry (implemented in amatrix-f6o):
  struct amatrix_mlx_sparse_entry { std::string key; mlx::core::array vals; mlx::core::array rows; mlx::core::array cols; int nrow; int ncol; int in_use; };
  static std::vector<amatrix_mlx_sparse_entry> mlx_sparse_registry;

  amatrix_mlx_sparse_store_bridge(key, values, p, i, dim):
    Build mlx::core::array for vals/rows/cols, eval(), cache in registry.
  amatrix_mlx_sparse_has_bridge(key): lookup → logical
  amatrix_mlx_sparse_drop_bridge(key): release entry
  amatrix_mlx_spmm_resident_bridge(sp_key, B_mat, trans_lhs_r):
    Lookup sp_key, call sparse_mm with cached arrays, return dense result.

### R layer (backends/amatrix.mlx/R/backend.R)
Add R wrappers for the 4 new bridges.
Add to backend object: sparse_resident_store/has/drop, spmm_resident.

### Residency integration (R/wrappers.R)
.amatrix_prepare_resident_arg already handles adgCMatrix for AF (amatrix-f6o).
Extend to also work for MLX backend: same logic, calls mlx sparse_store.
(The backend_name check already routes to the right backend.)

## Dependency
Requires amatrix-f6o (AF sparse residency) as structural reference — same R-level
wiring pattern, just C++ instead of C.

## Verification
  # Measure per-call cost flattens after first call (upload amortised)
  X = adgCMatrix(rsparsematrix(20000, 2000, density=0.01), preferred_backend='mlx')
  B = matrix(rnorm(2000), 2000, 1)
  t1 = system.time(for(i in 1:50) X %*% B)
  # Second run: all 50 calls should hit cache, not re-upload
  t2 = system.time(for(i in 1:50) X %*% B)
  cat('t1:', t1['elapsed'], 't2:', t2['elapsed'], '\n')
  # t2 should be noticeably faster than t1 (first run uploads, subsequent reuse)
  devtools::test()  # 1141+ pass

title: Add dot(x, y) inner product convenience function
description:
Named inner product drop(crossprod(x, y)) as a single function. Currently hot loops require verbose drop(crossprod(...)) or as.double(t(x) %*% y). Needed for GP log-likelihoods, gradient steps, residual norms.

Scope:
- dot(x, y) for vectors: returns scalar double = sum(x * y)
- dot(X, Y) for matrices: returns sum(X * Y) i.e. Frobenius inner product (trace of X'Y)
- GPU path: element-wise multiply then sum, staying resident
- Tests: matches sum(x*y) for vectors, matches sum(X*Y) for matrices, handles adgeMatrix inputs

title: amatrix.opencl: R backend object + registration + probe safety
description: Create R/backend.R with full backend object (matching MLX/AF signatures exactly, all _resident with defer param). R/zzz.R: conditional .onLoad, NOT eager. amatrix_opencl_enable_probe(register=TRUE). Coordinated core change: add opencl to .amatrix_optional_backend_specs() gated by amatrix.enable_opencl option. See docs/plan-amatrix-opencl.md.
notes: Phase 4 of 5. Depends on amatrix-6bi + amatrix-4te.

title: MLX sparse SpMM: GPU-accelerated sparse matmul on Apple Silicon
description:
## Problem
The MLX backend (backends/amatrix.mlx/) has no sparse support. adgCMatrix operations
route to the CPU backend even when MLX is active. On Apple Silicon (unified memory),
MLX sparse matmul avoids host→device copies entirely — the sparse CSR/CSC data is
already in unified memory.

## MLX sparse API
MLX exposes sparse operations via mlx::core::sparse_mm (C++) and mx.sparse (Python).
The relevant C++ call is:
  mlx::core::sparse_mm(sparse_A, dense_B, transpose_a, transpose_b, stream)
MLX's sparse format is CSR internally; dgCMatrix is CSC so we need a CSC→CSR conversion
or use t(X) trick (CSC of X = CSR of t(X)).

## Implementation sketch

### C++ bridge (amatrix_mlx_matmul.cpp or new amatrix_mlx_sparse.cpp)
- amatrix_mlx_spmm_bridge(values, p, i, dim, B, trans_lhs):
  - dgCMatrix CSC → MLX sparse (reinterpret CSC of X as CSR of t(X), then use trans flag)
  - mlx::core::sparse_mm + eval
  - Return dense R matrix result

### R layer (backends/amatrix.mlx/R/backend.R)
- amatrix_mlx_spmm() R wrapper
- supports(): accept adgCMatrix for matmul/crossprod/tcrossprod with NNZ threshold
  (option: amatrix.mlx.spmm_min_nnz, default 10000)
- matmul/crossprod/tcrossprod: guard on inherits(x, 'dgCMatrix'), route to spmm

## CSC→MLX sparse conversion note
MLX's sparse_mm expects the sparse argument in a specific format. The cleanest approach:
  CSC(X) = CSR(t(X))
So to compute X %*% B:  sparse_mm(t(X) as CSR, B, transpose_a=true)  [uses @i and @p directly]
To compute t(X) %*% B:  sparse_mm(t(X) as CSR, B, transpose_a=false) [no extra transpose]
This avoids any index conversion — dgCMatrix slots map directly to MLX CSR of t(X).

## Verification
- Correctness: compare against Matrix::crossprod(dgCMatrix, B) at tolerance 1e-4
- Performance: benchmark irlba(adgCMatrix, nv=10) on 20K×2K 2%-density matrix,
  CPU vs MLX; expect 2-5× speedup on M-series chips due to unified memory SpMV

title: Sparse norm and ewise: eliminate densification for scalar/structural ops
description:
## Problem
Several operations on adgCMatrix unnecessarily densify the matrix:

1. norm(adgCMatrix): S4 method dispatches to am_norm which calls as.matrix() first.
   Matrix::norm(dgCMatrix, type) handles '1', 'I', 'F', 'M' norms natively via sparse BLAS.

2. ewise scalar ops on adgCMatrix: unary ops like abs(X), sqrt(X), X * scalar work correctly
   via the Ops dispatch we have, but the CPU backend's ewise() calls do.call(op, list(lhs, ...))
   where lhs is a dgCMatrix — this actually works for most base R ops since they dispatch on
   dgCMatrix. However, the result is a dgCMatrix, not wrapped back as adgCMatrix. The rewrap
   in .amatrix_rewrap_like() may fail or densify depending on the op.

3. Sparse + dense broadcast (sweep/broadcast_ewise): sweep(adgCMatrix, 2, v, '*') densifies.
   For column-scaling (common in TF-IDF, data normalization), this can be done without
   densifying: scale column j of CSC sparse by v[j] — iterate NNZ in column j.

## Changes

### norm for sparse (R/wrappers.R, R/methods-sparse.R)
- setMethod('norm', 'adgCMatrix', function(x, type='1', ...) Matrix::norm(amatrix_materialize_host(x), type=type))
- Handles all types Matrix supports without densifying

### ewise scalar result rewrap (R/wrappers.R or R/backend-cpu.R)
- When ewise result is a sparseMatrix, wrap back as adgCMatrix via new_adgCMatrix()
  rather than adgeMatrix(), preserving sparse residency and backend preference
- Add test: abs(X_sp) stays adgCMatrix; X_sp * 2 stays adgCMatrix

### Sparse column/row scale without densification (R/wrappers.R)
- sweep.adgCMatrix: for MARGIN=2 (column scale, the common case), iterate CSC columns
  directly rather than as.matrix(). For MARGIN=1 (row scale), convert to CSR or densify.
- This keeps document-term matrices sparse through TF-IDF column normalization.

## Verification
- norm(adgCMatrix) matches Matrix::norm(as(x,'dgCMatrix')) for all 4 types
- abs(X_sp) class is adgCMatrix; X_sp * 2.0 class is adgCMatrix
- sweep(X_sp, 2, v, '*') for v of length ncol(X_sp): result is sparse, no memory spike
- devtools::test() still 1141+ pass

title: Expose am_eigh() public R wrapper for GPU symmetric eigendecomposition
description:
The mlx_linalg_eigh C bridge was implemented (amatrix-7ze) but there is no public am_eigh() R function. This is the linchpin for EMMA/FaST-LMM style variance component estimation: decompose K = Q Λ Q' once on GPU, then rotate y* = Q'y and X* = Q'X, reducing the LMM to a 1D profile optimisation over delta = sigma2_e/sigma2_b with only diagonal matrix ops in the inner loop.

Scope:
- Add am_eigh(K) to R/wrappers.R dispatching to the MLX eigh bridge (symmetric=TRUE, sorted eigenvalues ascending)
- Export in NAMESPACE; CPU fallback via base::eigen(x, symmetric=TRUE)
- Return named list(values=, vectors=) consistent with base::eigen
- Register setMethod eigen on adgeMatrix for the symmetric case
- Tests: eigenvalue reconstruction K ≈ Q diag(λ) Q', orthogonality Q'Q ≈ I, fast-mode MLX dispatch
close_reason: Implemented am_eigh() in R/wrappers.R as thin wrapper over am_eigen(symmetric=TRUE); MLX backend already dispatches to amatrix_mlx_eigh. 18 new tests pass.

title: sweep.adgCMatrix MARGIN=1 densifies for row-scaling
description:
## Location
R/wrappers.R:1010-1037 (sweep.adgCMatrix)

## Problem
The sparse sweep fast path only handles MARGIN=2 with * or /. MARGIN=1 (row-scaling/row-normalization) falls through to as.matrix(amatrix_materialize_host(x)) at line 1036, creating a full dense copy. Row-normalization (sweep(X, 1, rowNorms, "/")) is a very common data-science operation and preserves sparsity — each NNZ x[i,j] becomes x[i,j]/norms[i].

## Fix
Add MARGIN=1 fast path for * and /: iterate over @x slot, use @i to look up the row index, and apply STATS[row+1] to each value. O(nnz) time, O(1) extra memory.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] sweep(adgCMatrix, 1, stats, "*") does NOT call as.matrix()
- [ ] sweep(adgCMatrix, 1, stats, "/") does NOT call as.matrix()
- [ ] Result is adgCMatrix (sparsity preserved)
- [ ] Result matches sweep(as.matrix(X), 1, stats, "*") at tolerance 1e-14
- [ ] Benchmark: sweep(100K×10K sparse at 1%, 1, norms, "/") completes in <1s (dense would be >10s)
- [ ] MARGIN=1 with + or - still falls through to densification (correct behavior — destroys sparsity)

title: Add lu_factor() / lu_solve() cached LU factorization for non-SPD systems
description:
Mirrors chol_factor/chol_solve but for general (non-SPD) square matrices. Allows caching the LU factorization across multiple RHS solves, e.g. when solving (X'X + lambda*I) for many lambda values where the matrix is not guaranteed SPD.

Scope:
- lu_factor(A) returns an amLU object caching the factorization
- lu_solve(factor, B) solves A x = B for one or many RHS columns
- amLU S4 class with slots: factor (list with L, U, pivot), source_id, precision, backend
- Cache keyed by object_id (same pattern as chol_factor)
- CPU fallback via base::solve(); MLX backend via existing solve bridge
- Tests: lu_solve matches solve(A, B), cached vs uncached identical, non-square error

title: MLX solve_resident unnecessarily materializes both args to host
description:
## Location
backends/amatrix.mlx/R/backend.R:719+

## Problem
MLX solve_resident materializes both a_key and b_key to host R matrices, then calls the GPU chol_solve_bridge. This defeats the purpose of residency — two GPU→CPU transfers plus one CPU→GPU transfer on every solve call. The data is already on the Metal device and should stay there.

## Fix
Implement a native solve_resident bridge that operates on resident MLX array keys directly, or modify the existing chol_solve_bridge to accept resident keys. The ArrayFire backend already has solve_resident_bridge as a reference pattern.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] am_solve(A, b) with GPU-resident A and b does NOT call amatrix_materialize_host()
- [ ] The solve computation happens entirely on the Metal device
- [ ] Results match CPU solve reference at tolerance 1e-6 (f32 GPU)
- [ ] Benchmark: solve on GPU-resident 1000x1000 system is ≥1.5x faster than host fallback
- [ ] All existing tests pass

title: Sparse residency: cache AF sparse array across irlba Lanczos steps
description:
## Problem
irlba's Lanczos loop runs O(k) iterations, each doing two SpMV calls (A %*% v, t(A) %*% v).
Each call currently re-uploads the full sparse CSC array via af_create_sparse_array_from_ptr.
For a large sparse matrix (e.g. 100K×10K, 1M NNZ), the upload cost dominates the compute cost
and negates GPU speedup.

## Solution
Add a sparse-specific resident registry in the C layer, parallel to the existing dense registry.
Once an adgCMatrix is uploaded as an af_sparse_array, subsequent SpMM calls on the same matrix
reuse the cached device handle — zero host→device transfer cost on Lanczos steps 2…k.

## Implementation sketch

### C layer (arrayfire_bridge.c)
- Second registry: amatrix_af_sparse_entry { char* key; af_array sparse; int in_use }
- amatrix_arrayfire_sparse_store_bridge(key, values, p, i, dim): uploads CSC → af_sparse, caches
- amatrix_arrayfire_sparse_has_bridge(key): lookup
- amatrix_arrayfire_sparse_drop_bridge(key): release af_sparse + free key
- amatrix_arrayfire_spmm_resident_bridge(sp_key, B_mat, trans_lhs): lookup sp_key, call af_sparse_matmul

### R layer (backend.R)
- amatrix_arrayfire_sparse_store / sparse_has / sparse_drop / spmm_resident wrappers
- sparse_resident_store / sparse_resident_has / sparse_resident_drop / spmm_resident in backend object

### Dispatch (wrappers.R / residency.R)
- .amatrix_prepare_resident_arg: handle adgCMatrix — call sparse_store if not already resident
- .amatrix_try_resident_matmul / crossprod: detect sparse lhs → use spmm_resident bridge
- On first matmul(adgCMatrix, B), upload once; subsequent calls hit the cache
- irlba's repeated A %*% v loop gets zero-upload-cost SpMV from step 2 onward

## Correctness
- Sparse resident key uses same amatrix residency key mechanism as dense
- Release on GC / amatrix_gc() same as dense resident arrays
- Falls back to non-resident SpMM if upload fails

## Verification
- irlba(adgCMatrix, nv=50) on 50K×5K 1%-density matrix: verify sv correctness + timing shows
  per-iteration cost flat after step 1 (no re-upload latency)

ArrayFire thresholds are also too crude: dense checks max(dim), sparse checks nnz >= 10000.

## Scope

2. Route vector RHS through gemv path (not generic matmul)
3. Add RHS-width-aware thresholds (k=1 vs k=10 vs k=1000 have different GPU breakeven)

5. Disable sparse AF routing by default until calibrated (set spmm_min_nnz very high)
6. Calibrate each op class separately in amatrix_calibrate()

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Sparse routing disabled for AF by default (spmm_min_nnz = Inf or very high)

title: Add mat_sqrt(), mat_pow(), mat_log() matrix functions via eigh
description:
Matrix functions K^p for symmetric positive (semi-)definite matrices. All three are one-liners given eigh() which is already implemented.

  mat_sqrt(K)      → Q diag(sqrt(λ)) Q'   whitening, preconditioning, Mahalanobis
  mat_pow(K, p)    → Q diag(λ^p) Q'       fractional powers, regularization paths
  mat_log(K)       → Q diag(log(λ)) Q'    information geometry, kernel log-det gradients

Should warn/error when eigenvalues are negative (non-PSD input).

title: Sparse-aware covariance/correlation: formula identity avoids densification
description:
## Problem
covariance(adgCMatrix) currently centers X by computing X - mu, producing a dense n×p matrix
before calling crossprod. For a 100K×10K document-term matrix, this allocates 8GB just for
the centering step — negating every sparse optimization downstream.

## Algebraic fix
The one-pass identity avoids forming the centered matrix entirely:

  cov(X) = (X^T X) / (n-1) - n/(n-1) * mu * mu^T

where mu = colMeans(X) is a length-p vector.

- X^T X: already GPU-accelerated via SpMM (amatrix-vg0)
- mu: O(NNZ) via colSums(X) / n — already sparse-aware
- n/(n-1) * mu mu^T: p×p outer product on CPU (small)

Result: zero densification, O(NNZ + p^2) work instead of O(n*p + p^2).

## Implementation

### R/wrappers.R — am_covariance (find with grep -n 'am_covariance')
Add sparse guard at the top:
  if (inherits(host, 'sparseMatrix') || inherits(x, 'adgCMatrix')) {
    sp <- amatrix_materialize_host(x)  # dgCMatrix
    n  <- nrow(sp)
    mu <- Matrix::colMeans(sp)         # length p, O(NNZ)
    XtX <- as.matrix(crossprod(x))     # p×p via SpMM, GPU-accelerated
    d   <- if (is.null(denom)) n - 1L else as.integer(denom)
    if (isTRUE(center)) {
      return(XtX / d - (n / d) * tcrossprod(mu))
    }
    return(XtX / d)
  }

### correlation(adgCMatrix)
Same pattern: compute cov first, then diag-scale. Since cov stays dense (p×p),
the existing correlation code works without change after cov is fixed.

### AF/MLX backend covariance
AF backend's amatrix_arrayfire_covariance currently calls as.matrix(x) directly.
Add sparse guard: if inherits(x, 'dgCMatrix'), use formula identity.

## Verification
  set.seed(1); X = adgCMatrix(rsparsematrix(5000, 500, density=0.02))
  C1 = covariance(X)
  C2 = cov(as.matrix(amatrix_materialize_host(X)))
  cat('max err:', max(abs(C1 - C2)), '\n')  # should be < 1e-8
  # Also: correlation
  R1 = correlation(X)
  cat('correlation err:', max(abs(R1 - cor(as.matrix(amatrix_materialize_host(X))))), '\n')
  devtools::test()  # 1141+ pass

title: dense_lmm(): EMMA spectral LMM solver for dense covariance
description:
High-level model surface for linear mixed models where the random-effects covariance is a known dense SPD matrix K (genetic relatedness matrix, spatial kernel, etc.). Implements the EMMA/FaST-LMM spectral trick: decompose K once, reduce to 1D profile optimisation.

Depends on: amatrix-bg2 (am_eigh), amatrix-rrv (am_chol_logdet), amatrix-sdl (am_crossprod_weighted).

Algorithm:
1. am_eigh(K) -> Q, lambda  (GPU, done once)
2. Rotate: y* = Q'y, X* = Q'X  (GPU matmul)
3. Profile REML over delta = sigma2_e/sigma2_b via 1D Brent optimisation:
   - D(delta) = lambda + delta (diagonal)
   - beta(delta) = (X*' D^{-1} X*)^{-1} X*' D^{-1} y*
   - log-lik = -0.5 * (log|D| + (y* - X*beta)' D^{-1} (y* - X*beta))
4. Recover sigma2_e, sigma2_b, beta, BLUPs

API sketch:
  fit <- dense_lmm(y, X, K, backend='mlx', method='reml')
  fit$h2      # heritability / variance ratio
  fit$beta    # fixed effects
  fit$sigma2  # residual variance
  fit$loglik

Many-trait extension: dense_lmm(Y_many, X, K) reuses the single eigendecomposition across all traits — direct analogue of many_lm.

Target workloads: GWAS mixed model, spatial LMM, fMRI second-level with subject-level covariance.

New file: R/models-lmm.R
close_reason: Model surface belongs in a separate package. Core matrix primitives (am_eigh, am_chol_logdet, am_crossprod_weighted, am_xty_weighted, am_quad_form) are sufficient for an external package to implement efficient EMMA-style dense LMM. See am_rowscale/am_colscale issue for the one remaining gap.

title: Execution refactor: resident iterative substrate (no per-step S4 allocation)
description:
## Context
Iterative algorithms (PageRank, power iteration, CG, Sinkhorn) currently compose S4 ops at R level: each A %*% v allocates a new adgeMatrix via .amatrix_rewrap_value(). At ~5ms per allocation, a 1000-step loop wastes 5 seconds in wrapper churn alone. ## Scope
1. Formalize the mutable resident execution API: gemv_resident, spmv_resident, axpy_resident, scale_resident
2. Add in-place vector update kernels to MLX and AF backends
3. Build a .amatrix_iterative_context() helper that: uploads A once, provides gemv/axpy handles, cleans up on exit
4. CPU fast path: when backend is CPU, iterative context uses plain matrices/vectors with zero S4 overhead
5. Prove the substrate with one algorithm (pagerank or power_iter)

## Acceptance Criteria

title: Sparse QR wrapper: qr.Q/qr.R recurse infinitely on wrapped sparseQR
Algorithm migration: iterative workloads onto resident substrate
description:
## Context
Once the resident iterative substrate exists (previous bead), migrate real algorithms onto it. Each migration proves the substrate and delivers user-visible speedups.

## Scope (incremental, one at a time)
1. pagerank() — canonical sparse iterative: spmv + scale + convergence check
2. power_iter() — simplest eigenvalue iteration: gemv + normalize
3. sinkhorn() — optimal transport: alternating row/col normalization
4. block_lanczos refactor — migrate from current ad-hoc GPU plumbing to iterative_context
5. Model routines: IRLS (wls_fit iterative), coordinate descent, etc.

## Acceptance Criteria (per algorithm)
- [ ] Algorithm uses .amatrix_iterative_context(), not S4 dispatch per step
- [ ] GPU path beats base R by ≥2x on reference workload
- [ ] CPU path within 1.3x of base R
- [ ] Results match reference implementation at tolerance 1e-8
- [ ] Benchmark script in tools/ for regression testing

title: Bind helpers: preserve amatrix wrappers for cbind/rbind
Sparse→sparse ewise: preserve sparsity for scalar/unary ops on adgCMatrix
description:
## Problem
Unary and scalar ops on adgCMatrix (abs(X), X * 2, X + 0.1, -X) should return
adgCMatrix when the result is naturally sparse, but currently return adgeMatrix
(dense) because .amatrix_rewrap_like() doesn't detect the sparse case for all
code paths.

amatrix-9he (completed) fixed the ewise rewrap for the main Ops path. This issue
covers edge cases and adds systematic test coverage.

## Cases to handle

### 1. Unary ops: abs(X), -X, sign(X)
abs(dgCMatrix) → dgCMatrix (Matrix pkg handles natively)
-dgCMatrix → dgCMatrix
sign(dgCMatrix) → dgCMatrix (only at non-zeros)
These go through the Ops generic. Verify .amatrix_rewrap_like() wraps them as adgCMatrix.

### 2. Scalar multiply/divide: X * c, X / c
X * 2 → dgCMatrix (scale all non-zeros, keep structure)
X / 2 → dgCMatrix
These go through Ops(adgCMatrix, numeric). Already handled by methods-sparse.R Ops methods.
Check result class after dispatch.

### 3. X + scalar (breaks sparsity): X + 0.1
X + 0.1 → dense (every element changes) — this is CORRECT to densify.
Verify the behaviour is intentional and add a comment explaining why.

### 4. X * Y where both sparse (Hadamard)
adgCMatrix * adgCMatrix → dgCMatrix (intersection of non-zeros, stays sparse)
Matrix pkg handles Ops(dgCMatrix, dgCMatrix) natively.
After amatrix-9he rewrap fix, check this returns adgCMatrix not adgeMatrix.

## Systematic test file
Add tests/testthat/test-sparse-ewise.R:
  library(Matrix)
  X  = adgCMatrix(rsparsematrix(500, 200, density=0.05))
  X2 = adgCMatrix(rsparsematrix(500, 200, density=0.03))

  test_that('abs stays adgCMatrix', expect_s4_class(abs(X), 'adgCMatrix'))
  test_that('negation stays adgCMatrix', expect_s4_class(-X, 'adgCMatrix'))
  test_that('scalar multiply stays adgCMatrix', expect_s4_class(X * 2, 'adgCMatrix'))
  test_that('scalar divide stays adgCMatrix', expect_s4_class(X / 2, 'adgCMatrix'))
  test_that('hadamard stays adgCMatrix', expect_s4_class(X * X2, 'adgCMatrix'))
  test_that('X + scalar is dense (fill)', expect_false(is(X + 0.1, 'adgCMatrix')))
  test_that('values correct', {
    expect_equal(as.matrix(abs(X)), abs(as.matrix(amatrix_materialize_host(X))))
  })

## Verification
  devtools::test()  # new tests + 1141+ pass total

title: Randomized SVD on ArrayFire (cross-backend rsvd parity)
GPU sparse segment_sum/rowsum: grouped reduction without densification
description:
## Problem
rowsum(adgCMatrix, group) currently calls base::rowsum(as.matrix(host), ...) which
densifies the matrix first. For large sparse matrices (e.g. 100K×10K document-term),
densification blows up memory by 100× before the reduction even runs.

This matters for k-means and LDA on sparse data: the centroid-update step is
  centroids <- rowsum(X_sp, labels) / counts
which is O(NNZ) work but O(n*p) memory with the current dense path.

## ArrayFire approach (amatrix-f6o dependency recommended)
ArrayFire has no native sparse segment_sum, but we can implement it efficiently
using the sparse CSC structure:

For each non-zero (i, j, v) in X (row i, col j, value v):
  result[labels[i], j] += v

This is a scatter-add by row label over the sparse NNZ entries. Two approaches:

Option A: Pure R over sparse NNZ (no GPU, but O(NNZ) not O(n*p))
  - Iterate over CSC columns, for each NNZ entry scatter to result[labels[row], col]
  - C implementation in main package src/ for speed: ~O(NNZ) with no densification
  - No GPU dependency; works on CPU-only installs

Option B: GPU scatter via ArrayFire (requires amatrix-f6o sparse residency)
  - Upload NNZ values + row-label mapping to GPU
  - af_scan_by_key or custom scatter kernel
  - Only beneficial when NNZ is very large (> 5M)

## Recommended: Option A first, Option B later
A C-level sparse segment_sum (O(NNZ) scatter-add) is:
- Correct for all backends (CPU, MLX, ArrayFire)
- No memory spike (result is K×p dense, not n×p)
- Simple: just iterate CSC non-zeros

### C implementation (src/block_reorth.c or new src/sparse_ops.c)
  SEXP am_sparse_segment_sum_c(SEXP values, SEXP p, SEXP i_idx, SEXP dim, SEXP labels, SEXP K)
  - Allocate K×ncol result matrix (dense, typically small)
  - Loop over CSC columns j, non-zeros sp in p[j]..p[j+1]:
      result[labels[i_idx[sp]]-1, j] += values[sp]
  - Return R matrix

### R wiring (R/wrappers.R)
  rowsum.adgCMatrix: instead of as.matrix(host), call .Call('am_sparse_segment_sum_c', ...)
  Preserves group ordering logic already in rowsum.adgCMatrix.

### NAMESPACE
  Register am_sparse_segment_sum_c via useDynLib

## Verification
- rowsum(adgCMatrix, labels) == rowsum(as.matrix(adgCMatrix), labels) for all group types
- No densification: object.size stays O(NNZ + K*p) not O(n*p)
- Performance: rowsum on 50K×5K 1%-density matrix: C path should be 10-50× faster than
  as.matrix() path (avoids 50K*5K*8 bytes = 2GB allocation)
- devtools::test() still passes

.amatrix_model_dense_arg forces densification of all sparse inputs
description:
## Location
R/models-lm.R:1-27

## Problem
.amatrix_model_dense_arg is the gateway for ~15 exported functions (lm_fit, many_lm, pca, crossprod_weighted, rowscale, colscale, sym, am_diag, etc.). It unconditionally converts any sparse matrix to dense via as.matrix(amatrix_materialize_host(x)) → as_adgeMatrix. A 100K×10K sparse matrix at 1% density occupies ~80MB sparse but 8GB dense.

## Fix
Create .amatrix_model_sparse_or_dense_arg that preserves sparsity. Modify model functions that can work with sparse BLAS (via Matrix::crossprod, Matrix::solve, etc.) to use the new function. Functions that genuinely require dense (e.g., eigen decomposition of the full matrix) keep the current dense path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] New .amatrix_model_sparse_or_dense_arg function preserves adgCMatrix / dgCMatrix inputs
- [ ] lm_fit(sparse_X, y) via "normal" method uses Matrix::crossprod for X'X without densifying X
- [ ] object.size of X is unchanged after calling lm_fit (no hidden dense copy)
- [ ] lm_fit results match dense reference at tolerance 1e-10
- [ ] All existing tests pass

title: Sparse benchmark: end-to-end demo for k-means, LDA, PCA on large sparse matrices
description:
## Problem
There is no benchmark script demonstrating sparse GPU acceleration end-to-end.
Users cannot see where GPU helps for sparse workloads (document-term, genomics,
graph Laplacian) compared to CPU Matrix pkg sparse BLAS.

## Script: tools/benchmark-sparse.R

Structure mirrors benchmark-lda.R and benchmark-kmeans.R:
1. Load amatrix + available backends (MLX, ArrayFire)
2. Generate realistic sparse matrices:
   - Document-term: 20K docs × 50K terms, density 0.001 (TF-IDF style)
   - Genomics count: 5K cells × 20K genes, density 0.05
   - Graph Laplacian: 10K nodes, degree ~10 (density 0.001)
3. Run each algorithm under each backend, report timing + speedup vs CPU:

### Algorithms to benchmark
- truncated PCA via irlba(X, nv=50): measures SpMV throughput across Lanczos steps
  — shows benefit of sparse residency (amatrix-f6o)
- k-means (10 clusters, 20 iters): centroid update via rowsum(X, labels)
  — shows benefit of C sparse segment_sum (amatrix-m7k)  
- LDA (5 classes): Sw via crossprod(X) + class means via rowsum
  — exercises both SpMM and sparse rowsum
- Covariance sweep: cov(X) via formula identity
  — shows zero-densification path

### Correctness checks
For each algorithm: compare adgCMatrix result to base-R reference at tolerance 1e-4.

### Output format
Match existing benchmark scripts: ASCII table of median times + speedup ratios.
Add a summary section explaining crossover points (when GPU wins vs CPU).

## Additional: memory spike detector
Add a simple helper that checks object.size of intermediates to confirm no
densification occurs (O(NNZ) footprint throughout).

## Verification
  Rscript tools/benchmark-sparse.R
  # Should print correctness OK for all algos
  # Should show timing for all available backends

Reserve slot AFTER successful alloc (fix AF ghost-entry pattern). See docs/plan-amatrix-opencl.md.

title: amatrix_explain(x, op): human-readable dispatch diagnostic with actionable suggestions
Design question: idiomatic R syntax for fused GEMM
description:
## Problem

We want users to write natural R and have amatrix transparently call a single fused GEMM (BLAS DGEMM: C ← α·A·B + β·C) instead of separate matmul + add operations. The question is how far we can get with pure S4 dispatch before we must either (a) expose an explicit am_gemm() function or (b) introduce deferred/lazy evaluation.

## What a fused GEMM buys

A single BLAS DGEMM call takes α, β, transA, transB, and an accumulator C in one round-trip. On GPU this matters because separate kernels mean separate dispatches and a read-modify-write cycle on C. MLX composes mlx_matmul + scaled mlx_add; ArrayFire has native af_gemm.

## Current state

- `%*%` dispatches via S4 setMethod to am_matmul() and returns an adgeMatrix immediately — no laziness.
- `t()` is setMethod("t", "adgeMatrix") → am_transpose() → **materializes to host, transposes in R, re-wraps** (wrappers.R:400). So t(A) %*% B currently incurs a full device→host→device round-trip before the matmul starts.
- No am_gemm() exists yet.

## Option A: Zero-copy t() + explicit am_gemm()

Fix t() to return a thin transposed_adgeMatrix subclass (a new S4 object with a transposed=TRUE slot, sharing the same device buffer). %*% dispatches on transposed_adgeMatrix → crossprod path, no copy.

For the accumulator case, expose am_gemm(A, B, C=NULL, alpha=1, beta=1, transA=FALSE, transB=FALSE) as an explicit power-user function.

User writes:

    t(A) %*% B          # → crossprod, zero copy, elegant
    am_gemm(A, B, C, alpha=2, beta=0.5, transA=TRUE)  # explicit for full signature

Tradeoffs:
- t() fix is independently correct and worth doing regardless
- am_gemm() is honest — users who need BLAS semantics ask for them explicitly
- am_gemm() is not idiomatic R; breaks the "looks like base R" goal

## Option B: scaled_adgeMatrix wrapper for alpha

Additionally intercept scalar multiplication: s * A returns a scaled_adgeMatrix(A, scalar=s). Then %*% on scaled_adgeMatrix extracts alpha before calling the backend.

User writes:

    2 * t(A) %*% B       # → am_matmul(A, B, alpha=2, transA=TRUE), no am_gemm needed

But beta/accumulator still requires am_gemm() or deferred eval, because by the time + is evaluated, the matmul result is already a plain adgeMatrix — there is no type-level signal that it was a matmul.

Tradeoffs:
- Handles alpha naturally
- 2 * A no longer returns an adgeMatrix; surprises users who pass it to other functions
- Adds a new public-ish class with coercion obligations
- Only covers alpha; beta still needs explicit am_gemm() for the full signature

## Option C: pending_matmul deferred class

%*% returns a lazy pending_matmul list. Arithmetic operators accumulate alpha/beta/C. Forced on as.matrix(), print(), or when passed to a non-amatrix consumer.

User writes:

    2 * t(A) %*% B + 0.5 * C   # → single am_gemm call at force time

This is the only option that captures the full GEMM signature from natural R.

Tradeoffs:
- Breaks the current S4 public contract: %*% is documented to return adgeMatrix; existing tests assert this
- adgeMatrix/adgCMatrix are S4 classes; pending_matmul is S3 — the dispatch model would need to bridge both
- t() as identity mutation is unsafe: object_id keys the residency registry and factor caches (QR, Cholesky, SVD). If t(A) shares identity with A, cached factor state silently corrupts
- Incomplete: print(), dim(), nrow(), Ops, and every generic that expects a matrix would need coercion guards
- "Passing to non-amatrix function forces evaluation" is correct in theory but hard to guarantee in practice

## The hard constraint

The beta/accumulator case (A %*% B + beta * C) cannot be fused without deferred evaluation. R evaluates %*% before + sees the result. At the point + is dispatched, both operands are plain adgeMatrix objects with no record of how they were produced. There is no pure-S4 mechanism to recover the matmul provenance.

## Key question for external input

Given:
1. The PRD commits to Matrix-compatible S4 semantics and "boring and eager-feeling" objects
2. The performance goal is to beat torch on shared-X many-Y workloads (not generic dense GEMM)
3. The accumulator pattern (beta*C) is rare in idiomatic R; alpha != 1 is also uncommon outside BLAS tutorials

Is the complexity of Option B or C justified, or is Option A (zero-copy t() + explicit am_gemm()) the right call? Is there a fourth option we have not considered?

title: rowmeans/colmeans always materialize to CPU, ignore sparse and GPU
description:
## Location
R/wrappers.R:829-836

## Problem
rowmeans() and colmeans() unconditionally call as.matrix(amatrix_materialize_host(x)). For adgCMatrix, this densifies the entire sparse matrix. For GPU-resident adgeMatrix, this pulls data to CPU unnecessarily. Matrix::rowMeans and Matrix::colMeans work natively on dgCMatrix. GPU path can compose via existing rowsums()/colsums().

## Fix
1. Sparse guard: if inherits(host, "sparseMatrix"), use Matrix::rowMeans/Matrix::colMeans
2. GPU composition: rowmeans(x) = rowsums(x) / nrow(x), colmeans(x) = colsums(x) / ncol(x) — both rowsums/colsums already have GPU resident paths

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] rowMeans(adgCMatrix) does NOT call as.matrix()
- [ ] rowMeans(GPU-resident adgeMatrix) uses rowsums GPU path, no host materialization
- [ ] Results match base::rowMeans reference at tolerance 1e-14
- [ ] colMeans same criteria
- [ ] All existing tests pass

title: Add batch_chol() and batch_solve() for parallel small-matrix factorizations
description:
Many statistical workflows require factorizing many small matrices in parallel rather than one large one: GWAS LD windows, bootstrap replicates, per-subject Cholesky factors, searchlight analyses. MLX supports batched BLAS natively (input shape [B, n, n]). Currently requires a slow R for-loop.

API:
  batch_chol(A)      # A is [B, n, n] array or list of n×n matrices → list/array of amChol
  batch_solve(L, B)  # L from batch_chol, B is [B, n, k] → [B, n, k] solutions
  batch_crossprod(A) # A is [B, n, p] → [B, p, p] array of X'X matrices

Backend:
- MLX: mlx_linalg_cholesky on batched array in one kernel call
- CPU fallback: lapply over list elements

Use case example (bootstrap):
  Ks <- array(dim=c(B, n, n))  # B bootstrap kernel matrices
  Ls <- batch_chol(Ks)
  alphas <- batch_solve(Ls, Y_rep)  # solve all B systems at once

title: Add rowmeans() and colmeans() row/column mean reductions
description:
Direct parallel to existing rowsums/colsums. Currently missing despite being one of the most common matrix operations. Required for centering, normalization, and any preprocessing pipeline that avoids breaking GPU residency via apply().

Scope:
- rowmeans(X, na.rm=FALSE) — row means, returns vector or adgeMatrix
- colmeans(X, na.rm=FALSE) — column means, returns vector or adgeMatrix
- Register setMethod rowMeans/colMeans on adgeMatrix and adgCMatrix (S4 dispatch like rowSums/colSums)
- CPU fallback via base::rowMeans/colMeans
- Tests: matches base R, dimensions correct, na.rm respected

title: Add am_chol_logdet() log-determinant from amChol factor
description:
Every REML log-likelihood evaluation needs log|V| = 2 * sum(log(diag(L))). Currently users must reach into factor@factor manually. This trivial omission blocks writing clean REML objectives on top of amatrix.

Scope:
- Add am_chol_logdet(factor) to R/chol-factor.R alongside am_chol_solve()
- Export in NAMESPACE
- Accepts an amChol object, returns a scalar double
- Also consider am_chol_diag(factor) returning diag(L) as a convenience
- Tests: logdet matches log(det(K)) for small SPD matrices, matches 2*sum(log(diag(chol(K))))

title: Add solve_triangular(L, B, lower=TRUE) explicit triangular solve
description:
Explicit trsm-level triangular solve. Currently triangular solves are only accessible through chol_solve (which requires an amChol wrapper). Callers who already have a raw upper/lower triangular factor R (e.g., from qr.R(), a custom factorization, or the factor slot of amChol) cannot access the fast triangular solve path directly.

API:
  solve_triangular(R, B, lower = FALSE)  # upper triangular by default (Cholesky convention)
  solve_triangular(L, B, lower = TRUE)   # lower triangular

Backend:
  CPU: backsolve() / forwardsolve()
  MLX: mlx triangular solve bridge (same path as chol_solve internals)

Tests: solve_triangular(R, R %*% x) ≈ x for known x, lower=TRUE variant,
  multiple RHS columns.

title: ewise() eagerly materializes both args before trying GPU resident path
description:
## Location
R/wrappers.R:1317-1318

## Problem
`ewise()` calls `.amatrix_host_arg(e1)` and `.amatrix_host_arg(e2)` unconditionally BEFORE attempting the resident GPU path at lines 1321-1326. If the resident path succeeds, the host materializations are wasted — a full GPU→CPU transfer per operand that is never used. This is the hottest path in the package (every +, -, *, / on amatrices).

## Fix
Defer `host_e1`/`host_e2` computation into the fallback/cold branch. Only materialize if `.amatrix_try_resident_ewise()` returns NULL.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `ewise()` does NOT call `.amatrix_host_arg()` before `.amatrix_try_resident_ewise()`
- [ ] When both operands are GPU-resident, zero GPU→CPU transfers occur during ewise
- [ ] Existing ewise tests still pass (1156+)
- [ ] Benchmark: `X + Y` on two 5000x1000 GPU-resident matrices is ≥1.5x faster than before

title: Add am_crossprod_weighted(X, w) fused weighted normal equations
description:
Computes X'WX where W = diag(w) without materializing the n×n diagonal or the n×p intermediate. Currently wls_fit does x_host * sqrt_w then crossprod, allocating a full copy. For large n (neuroimaging, GWAS) this dominates memory bandwidth. This is the critical primitive for PIRLS-style iterations in dense LMMs.

Scope:
- am_crossprod_weighted(X, w) — X' diag(w) X, returns p×p matrix
- am_tcrossprod_weighted(X, w) — X diag(w) X', returns n×n matrix (use with care for large n)
- am_xty_weighted(X, w, y) — X' diag(w) y, returns p×k matrix (weighted cross-moment)
- Backend: MLX can fuse via element-wise scale then matmul in one resident pass; CPU fallback is the sqrt-scale trick
- Migrate wls_fit internals to use this path
- Tests: matches manual diag(w) %*% X path, wls_fit results unchanged
close_reason: Implemented am_crossprod_weighted(X,w) and am_xty_weighted(X,w,y) in R/wrappers.R using sqrt-scale trick. 18 new tests pass.

title: Fix benchmark units and add warm-path variant
description: median_ms column is likely µs (bench_time ns × 1e3). Verify and fix label. Add warm variant that pre-uploads before timing to show real GPU compute speedup. Files: tools/benchmark-regression.R. Acceptance: correct units; warm path shows >2x MLX at medium/large sizes for matmul/crossprod; baseline.csv updated.

description: am_dist(X, tile_size=N) for n>50k; auto-tiles when nrow(X)>50000. Implemented in R/wrappers.R.

title: Add sym(A) symmetry enforcement (A + t(A)) / 2
description:
Enforces symmetry via (A + t(A)) / 2. After floating-point accumulation in crossprod or Cholesky paths, SPD matrices drift asymmetric. Standard fix before chol_factor() on a computed matrix.

Scope:
- sym(A) returns (A + t(A)) / 2 as adgeMatrix preserving backend/precision
- GPU path: keep on device without host round-trip
- Tests: result is exactly symmetric, matches manual (A+t(A))/2, idempotent sym(sym(A))==sym(A)
close_reason: sym(A) added in R/wrappers.R as (A + t(A))/2, idempotent, preserves backend/precision. 957 tests pass.

title: Add norm(X, type) matrix and vector norms with GPU dispatch
description:
S4 setMethod on adgeMatrix for base R norm(). GPU-dispatched norms avoid materializing large matrices for what is essentially a reduction.

Types to support:
- 'F' / 'f': Frobenius norm = sqrt(sum(X^2)) — GPU-friendly reduction
- '1': max column sum (max of colsums of abs values)
- 'I': max row sum (max of rowsums of abs values)
- '2': largest singular value (expensive; dispatch to rsvd for large matrices)
- default: same as base R norm()

Scope:
- Register setMethod('norm', 'adgeMatrix', ...)
- Frobenius and 1/I norms via resident rowsums/colsums paths
- CPU fallback for '2' norm below rsvd threshold
- Tests: matches base::norm() for all types on both small and large matrices

title: am_addmm: scaled matrix multiply primitive (alpha*A%*%B + beta*C)
description:
Implement am_addmm(A, B, C=NULL, alpha=1, beta=0) as a standard BLAS-3 primitive.

Semantics: alpha * (A %*% B) + beta * C
- C=NULL treated as zeros (pure scaled matmul)
- Returns adgeMatrix, resident if A is resident
- Subsumes: gemm, K-means -2 scaling, ridge update patterns

MLX: mlx_addmm (ops.h:37-44) directly. C=NULL -> mlx_zeros of shape {nrow(A), ncol(B)}.
AF: af_matmul + af_mul(alpha) + af_add(beta*C), chained async.

Follows same resident pattern as matmul: _prepare_resident_arg for A,B,C; out_key param; return adgeMatrix with binding.

Files: amatrix_mlx_matmul.c, arrayfire_bridge.c, init.c (both), backends/*/R/backend.R, R/wrappers.R, NAMESPACE

Quality gates:
- am_addmm(A, B, alpha=-2) matches (-2)*A%*%B
- am_addmm(A, B, C, alpha=0.5, beta=1) matches 0.5*A%*%B + C
- Resident output verified

Depends on: am_pairwise_sqdist_argmin uses am_addmm internally once available.

title: arrayfire: af::getActiveBackend() C bridge + .onLoad() startup probe
notes: New C bridge: amatrix_arrayfire_active_backend_bridge calls af::getActiveBackend() and returns string 'CUDA'/'OpenCL'/'CPU'. Add .onLoad() in R/zzz.R: if native unavailable, print install hint; if available, print active backend. Prerequisite: amatrix-ptx.

title: Harden deferred dense coercion when resident data is missing
description: Context: deferred adgeMatrix still stores a NaN sentinel and as.numeric/as.vector on a deferred object with lost resident state returns NaN with a warning. Public deferred mode is currently disabled, but the internal constructor remains footgun-prone. Scope: either make lost-resident deferred coercion fail loudly with a deterministic error or provide a safe host fallback path, and add regression tests around as.matrix/as.numeric/as.vector on deferred objects. Acceptance: deferred host coercion no longer silently returns NaN payloads when resident state is gone.

title: GPU sparse support: ArrayFire SpMM bridge
description: Follow-on to CPU sparse phase. adgCMatrix operations (matmul/crossprod/tcrossprod) currently route through Matrix pkg sparse BLAS on CPU. For GPU acceleration, need: (1) C bridge in arrayfire_bridge.c for af_sparse_from_host / af_sparse_matmul, (2) ArrayFire backend sparse-aware matmul/crossprod dispatch, (3) residency tracking for sparse device arrays. CPU path remains fallback when ArrayFire SpMM not available. Reference: ArrayFire af::sparse docs.

title: KronMatrix: structured Kronecker A⊗B class avoiding O(p^2 q^2) materialization
Add crossprod_add_diag(X, lambda) fused X'X + lambda*I
description:
Fused computation of X'X + lambda*I without materializing X'X as an intermediate. Critical hot path for ridge regression, kernel ridge, GP posterior, and any regularized normal equations. Currently requires two ops and a host round-trip.

API:
  crossprod_add_diag(X, lambda)     # X'X + lambda * I  (p×p result)
  crossprod_add_diag(X, d)          # X'X + diag(d) when d is a vector (diagonal regularization)

Backend:
  GPU: compute crossprod then fuse diagonal add in one resident pass
  CPU fallback: crossprod(X) + lambda * diag(ncol(X))

Common pattern:
  L <- chol_factor(crossprod_add_diag(X, lambda))  # ridge in one line

title: arrayfire: af::solve() and af::cholesky() C bridges (GPU-native path)
notes: Implement two C bridges: amatrix_arrayfire_solve_resident_bridge (calls af::solve) and amatrix_arrayfire_chol_resident_bridge (calls af::cholesky). Remove the current R-level CPU fallback in amatrix_arrayfire_solve_resident/chol_resident. Add 'solve' and 'chol' to amatrix_arrayfire_capabilities(). Prerequisite: amatrix-ptx.
close_reason: done: solve_bridge and chol_bridge implemented in arrayfire_bridge.c, solve+chol in capabilities

title: amatrix_warm(): pre-compile GPU kernels to eliminate cold-start latency
Weighted helpers force GPU→CPU→GPU round-trip for row-scaling
description:
## Location
R/wrappers.R:674-718 (crossprod_weighted, tcrossprod_weighted, xty_weighted)
R/models-lm.R:437 (.amatrix_apply_row_weights)

## Problem
All weighted helpers materialize X to CPU, scale by sqrt(weights) on CPU, then rewrap and call am_crossprod which re-uploads to GPU. This is a full GPU→CPU→GPU round-trip on every weighted operation. For 10000x500 matrices this is ~80MB wasted bandwidth each direction.

## Fix
Replace the CPU scaling step with broadcast_ewise_resident(X_key, sqrt_w, margin=1, "*") to keep data on GPU, then chain directly to crossprod_resident. The broadcast_ewise_resident op already exists in both AF and MLX backends.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `crossprod_weighted(X, w)` on a GPU-resident X does NOT call `amatrix_materialize_host()`
- [ ] The scaling step uses `broadcast_ewise_resident` or equivalent GPU path
- [ ] Result matches CPU reference at tolerance 1e-10
- [ ] Same fix applied to `tcrossprod_weighted`, `xty_weighted`, `.amatrix_apply_row_weights`
- [ ] Benchmark: `crossprod_weighted` on GPU-resident 5000x500 matrix is ≥2x faster than before

title: am_segment_sum / am_segment_mean: GPU grouped-reduction primitives
description:
Implement am_segment_sum(X, labels, K) and am_segment_mean(X, labels, K) as first-class backend primitives.

Semantics:
- am_segment_sum: K×p matrix, row k = sum of X rows where labels==k
- am_segment_mean: K×p matrix, row k = mean of X rows where labels==k
- Both return GPU-resident adgeMatrix when X is resident (Phase 3 absorbed)
- Division (for mean), counts, and zero-group handling all happen on GPU

MLX: mlx_scatter_add_axis (ops.h:907) replaces O(K*n*p) one-hot matmul with O(n*p) scatter. Counts as MLX_INT32 array; divide via mlx_divide before single sync. Store result as resident, return dims only.

AF: keep one-hot matmul but add out_key for resident output; division on AF via af_div.

CPU fallback: loop in R/backend-cpu.R.

Files: amatrix_mlx_matmul.c, arrayfire_bridge.c, init.c (both), backends/*/R/backend.R, R/wrappers.R, NAMESPACE

Quality gates:
- Equivalence vs CPU at 1e-4 (GPU) / 1e-10 (CPU backend)
- Edge cases: empty cluster, K=1, all-same labels, wide p, large K, unsorted labels, non-contiguous resident
- 0 test failures

title: adgCMatrix preferred_backend enforcement in sparse dispatch
description:
## Problem
When a user creates adgCMatrix(X, preferred_backend='mlx'), the sparse dispatch
in supports() only checks NNZ threshold — it ignores the preferred_backend slot.
The matrix ends up routed to whichever GPU backend registered first rather than
the one the user requested. This is inconsistent with how adgeMatrix respects
preferred_backend.

## Current behaviour
In .amatrix_backend_for() (R/backend-planning.R), preferred_backend is used as a
hint for adgeMatrix. For adgCMatrix, the supports() check in the AF backend uses
is(x, 'adgCMatrix') without checking x@preferred_backend — so AF wins even if
the user said 'mlx'.

## Fix

### R/backend-planning.R — .amatrix_backend_for()
The existing logic for preferred_backend should already apply if supports() checks
are gated correctly. Verify that .amatrix_backend_for() passes preferred_backend
preference for adgCMatrix the same way it does for adgeMatrix.
  grep -n 'preferred_backend\|backend_for' R/backend-planning.R | head -30

### R/backend*.R — supports() for sparse
Each backend's supports() for adgCMatrix should add a preference check:
  pref <- x@preferred_backend
  if (!is.null(pref) && nzchar(pref) && pref != backend_name) return(FALSE)
This mirrors the adgeMatrix pattern.

### Fallback order
If preferred_backend='mlx' but NNZ < threshold, fall back to CPU (Matrix sparse BLAS),
not to the ArrayFire backend. Currently the fallback may pick AF if it's registered.

## Verification
  options(amatrix.arrayfire.available=TRUE)
  amatrix_arrayfire_register(); amatrix_mlx_register()  # if available
  X_af  = adgCMatrix(rsparsematrix(5000,500,density=0.05), preferred_backend='arrayfire')
  X_mlx = adgCMatrix(rsparsematrix(5000,500,density=0.05), preferred_backend='mlx')
  B = matrix(rnorm(500*10),500,10)
  info_af  = amatrix_execution_info(X_af  %*% B)
  info_mlx = amatrix_execution_info(X_mlx %*% B)
  stopifnot(info_af  == 'arrayfire')
  stopifnot(info_mlx == 'mlx')
  devtools::test()  # 1141+ pass

title: am_pairwise_sqdist_argmin: fused nearest-centroid assignment primitive
description:
Implement am_pairwise_sqdist_argmin(X, Ct, x_norms, c_norms) as a general-purpose nearest-neighbor primitive.

Semantics:
- X: n×p resident adgeMatrix (query set)
- Ct: p×K matrix (reference set, transposed)
- x_norms: n-vector ||xi||^2
- c_norms: K-vector ||ck||^2
- Returns: integer vector length n, 1-indexed nearest reference per query row

Applications: K-means assignment, brute-force kNN inner loop, prototype search, any distance-based assignment.

MLX: single unevaluated graph — mlx_addmm(zeros, X, Ct, alpha=-2) -> add x_norms {n,1} -> add c_norms {1,K} -> mlx_argmin_axis(axis=1) — single mlx_synchronize at end. Uses mlx_addmm from ops.h:37.

AF: chain af_matmul -> af_mul(-2) -> af_add (broadcast) -> af_imin. AF already async so these chain without intermediate syncs.

CPU fallback: max.col(-D) on CPU distance matrix.

Files: amatrix_mlx_matmul.c (new bridge, ~150 lines), arrayfire_bridge.c, init.c (both), backends/*/R/backend.R, R/wrappers.R, NAMESPACE

Quality gates:
- Matches max.col(-dist_matrix(X,C)) at 1e-4 (GPU) / 1e-10 (CPU)
- K=1, K=n, p=1, p=1000
- Both inputs resident; X resident Ct plain; both plain
